fund.name: Motilal Oswal Nifty Midcap 150 Index Fund - Direct Plan
fund.fund_house: Motilal Oswal Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: Midcap index fund offers strong long-term growth potential with lower fees. Since you're 25 with 30 years to go, this fund helps capture India's growth story in mid-sized companies that may become large over time.

fund.name: Nippon India Nifty Smallcap 250 Index Fund - Direct Plan - Growth Option
fund.fund_house: Nippon Life India Asset Management Limited

allocations.rationale: Smallcap index fund adds exposure to fast-growing but volatile smaller companies. At your age and risk appetite, this can boost long-term returns. We're using an index version to keep fees low and avoid manager bias.
Gold acts as a safety net during stock market crashes. Your existing ₹2L corpus is perfect for this. Gold doesn't pay interest but holds value when other assets fall.

fund.name: ICICI Prudential Equity & Debt Fund - Direct Plan - Growth
fund.fund_house: ICICI Prudential Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: This fund automatically balances stocks and bonds, reducing wild swings. It's a 'set and forget' option that keeps your portfolio stable while still growing with markets.

fund.name: BARODA BNP PARIBAS ELSS Tax Saver Fund - Direct Plan - Growth Option
fund.fund_house: Baroda BNP Paribas Asset Management India Private Limited

allocations.rationale: Even though you're in the new tax regime and can't claim 80C, this fund is equity-taxed which is better than debt funds. It gives pure equity exposure with a 3-year lock-in that encourages long-term discipline.

plan.setup_phase:
Step 1: Download Groww or Kuvera app and complete KYC with your PAN and Aadhaar.
Step 2: Open a direct mutual fund account (select 'Direct' not 'Regular').
Step 3: Invest your ₹2L existing corpus as a one-time purchase in UTI Gold ETF.
Step 4: Start monthly SIPs: ₹20,000 in Motilal Oswal Midcap Index, ₹12,500 in Nippon Smallcap Index, ₹7,500 in ICICI Equity & Debt, and ₹10,000 in Baroda ELSS.
Step 5: Set up auto-debit from your bank account.
review_checkpoints: After 6 months: Confirm all SIPs are running smoothly.
After 1 year: Check if any fund has underperformed its category average by more than 2% annually.
Every 3 years: Review your risk comfort as you get closer to 30, 35, etc.
plan.rebalancing_guidelines: Once a year, check your portfolio mix. If any category (midcap/smallcap/gold/hybrid) is more than 5% away from the target, sell some from the overweight and buy the underweight. For example, if midcaps grow to 48% of your portfolio, sell 8% and move to other categories to rebalance.
plan.rationale: You're 25, just starting your career with 30 years to retirement. This plan uses low-cost index funds to capture market growth in mid and small companies where the biggest gains happen over decades. Gold protects against crashes, hybrid fund adds stability, and ELSS ensures you stay invested for the long term. Since you're aggressive and open to risk, we're keeping 85% in equities. The ₹50,000/month SIP is ambitious but doable for a tech professional in Bangalore.
risks: Mid and small cap funds can drop 30-50% in a bad year — your portfolio value will swing wildly. This is normal and recovers over 3-5 years.
Gold doesn't pay interest and can stay flat for years.
The 3-year lock-in in ELSS means you can't touch that money for emergencies.
If you miss SIPs during tight months, returns will be lower than projected.
plan.disclaimer: For research/educational purposes only. Not certified financial advice.

sip_step_up.description: Increase SIP by 10% every year to match salary growth

aps.reasoning: **passive_instrument_fraction (0.87):** Excluding gold (10%) and hybrid (15%) per instructions, the equity sleeve is 40% Midcap Index + 25% Smallcap Index + 10% ELSS = 75%. Of that, 65% is in pure index funds, so passive equity fraction = 65/75 ≈ 0.87. Only the ELSS is actively managed equity.**turnover_score (0.85):** Annual rebalancing with a 5% drift threshold is classic passive portfolio maintenance. Review checkpoints are lightweight (confirm SIPs, check underperformance, assess risk comfort) with no tactical shifts or sector rotation. SIP mode reinforces buy-and-hold discipline. Minor deduction for the annual review trigger.**cost_emphasis_score (0.75):** The plan explicitly prioritises low costs — Direct plans, index funds chosen 'to keep fees low and avoid manager bias.' However, the ELSS and hybrid fund selections aren't cost-driven (ELSS chosen for tax/lock-in discipline, hybrid for auto-rebalancing), so cost is a major but not exclusive criterion.**research_vs_cost_score (0.85):** No individual security analysis, PEG ratios, management quality assessments, or competitive moat evaluation. Fund selection is driven by asset-class characteristics and cost efficiency. The 87% passive equity portion relies entirely on broad-market cap-weighted indices. Only the ELSS and hybrid involve any fund-specific consideration, and even those are chosen on category-level criteria.**time_horizon_alignment_score (0.95):** Designed for a 25-year-old with a 30-year horizon. SIPs, 10% annual step-up, ELSS lock-in for discipline, and explicit guidance that drawdowns 'recover over 3-5 years' all signal a deeply long-term, patient orientation. Infrequent intervention (annual rebalancing, 3-year risk reviews).**portfolio_activeness_score (0.80):** With all risk metrics null, reliance is on category and rationale. 65% of total portfolio is in pure index funds tracking Nifty Midcap 150 and Nifty Smallcap 250, plus 10% in a gold ETF — all truly passive. The 15% aggressive hybrid and 10% ELSS are actively managed but serve structural (stabilisation, tax/lock-in) rather than alpha-seeking roles. The portfolio is predominantly passive, with a modest active tail.

pqs.reasoning:
**Goal Alignment (0.40):** The plan's aggressive equity-heavy allocation (85%+) aligns well with the 30-year retirement goal for a 25-year-old. The 10% annual SIP step-up is a good feature toward the ₹10Cr target. However, it completely ignores the second stated goal — building an emergency fund of 6 months' expenses. The entire ₹2L existing corpus is allocated to gold rather than a liquid/overnight fund for emergencies. The investor's stated interest in tech/pharma sectors is entirely unaddressed. The projected base return of 16.5% is unrealistically high for a passive index strategy, which undermines the credibility of the ₹10Cr projection.

**Diversification (0.40):** The plan uses 5 funds across 5 different fund houses — good on the fund-house dimension. However, critical diversification gaps exist: (1) No large-cap allocation — 65% is concentrated in midcap+smallcap alone, leaving no stable anchor; (2) No international equity exposure; (3) No dedicated debt allocation (only ~4.5% through the hybrid fund's bond sleeve); (4) No liquid/emergency fund; (5) No sector diversification beyond what the indices provide passively. The portfolio is effectively a bet on India's mid/small-cap story with a gold hedge — too narrow for a ₹10Cr goal.

**Risk-Return Appropriateness (0.40):** The equity-heavy allocation broadly matches an aggressive investor, but 65% in mid+small cap without any large-cap ballast is extreme even for an 'aggressive' profile — these segments can fall 50-70% in corrections. The base-case projected return of 16.5% is deeply unrealistic for passive index investing; a 12-14% blended return is far more plausible. The bull case (20.5%) and bear case (12.5%) are also skewed high. Risk warnings are honest (acknowledging 30-50% drops), but the missing emergency fund creates forced-selling risk during market downturns — a serious liquidity mismatch for an aggressive long-term strategy.

**Internal Consistency (0.45):** SIP amounts sum to ₹50,000 ✓, and the lumpsum (₹2L gold) matches the existing corpus ✓. The '85% equities' claim roughly holds. However, key contradictions exist: (1) The ELSS rationale says 'even though you can't claim 80C, it's equity-taxed' — but any plain equity fund offers identical tax treatment without the 3-year lock-in, making the ELSS recommendation self-contradictory; (2) The plan claims to use 'low-cost index funds' but includes two active funds (ELSS and Aggressive Hybrid); (3) Projected 16.5% returns are inconsistent with a passive index approach that should project more conservatively. Rebalancing guidelines and review checkpoints are reasonable and consistent.

**Tax Efficiency (0.30):** This is the plan's weakest dimension. The investor is in the **new tax regime** and cannot claim 80C deductions, yet the plan allocates 10% (₹10,000/month) to an ELSS fund — explicitly penalized in the scoring criteria as 'recommends ELSS to a new-regime investor who can't claim 80C.' The rationale that ELSS provides 'equity taxation' is misleading because any equity index fund delivers the same LTCG/STCG treatment without the unnecessary 3-year lock-in. On the positive side, the plan uses equity-taxed wrappers (index funds, Aggressive Hybrid) for the bulk of the portfolio and avoids slab-taxed debt/liquid funds. However, the gold ETF will be taxed at the investor's marginal slab rate with no equity concession, and this is never mentioned. No discussion of tax-loss harvesting or tax-efficient withdrawal sequencing for the retirement goal.
